source_metadata.title_raw: Gordon F. Newton - A-82-064 - June 2, 1982 - Los Angeles

content.full_text:
A 22-064

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • •1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660         322-5901         322-6441         322-6444

June 2, 1982


Mr. Gordon F. Newton
Chairman, CCLA PAC
Coca-Cola Bottling Company
of Los Angeles
1334 So. Central Avenue
Los Angeles, CA  90021

Re:  Advice Request No. A-82-064

Dear Mr. Newton:

     In reviewing the letter I sent to you on May 24, 1982,
I realized I had not answered your questions regarding the
reporting of employee contributions to CCLA PAC that are
"earmarked" for a specific state or local candidate.

     CCLA PAC does not become a "committee" within the
meaning of the Political Reform Act with the attendant
reporting obligations if its only involvement with state or
local elections consists of acting as an intermediary or
conduit for contributions from employees which are
earmarked for specific state or local candidates or ballot
measures.  See Government Code Section 82013.  If CCLA
PAC's involvement in state or local elections extends
beyond this, you should contact us again for advice on your
reporting responsibilities.

     I have enclosed a copy of a previous advice letter on
this subject dated September 16, 1976, which explains the
reporting requirements of an intermediary under Government
Code Section 84302 and the distinction between an
intermediary and a conduit.

     If I can be of further assistance, please feel free to
contact me at 916/322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]

                                        Diane Maura Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

Enclosure

Advice

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322.5662        322.5660           322.5901           322.6441           322.6444

May 24, 1982

Mr. Gordon F. Newton
Chairman, CCLA PAC
Coca-Cola Bottling Company
of Los Angeles
1334 So. Central Avenue
Los Angeles, CA 90021

Re: Advice Request No. A-82-064

Dear Mr. Newton:

Thank you for your letter requesting advice from this office on behalf of Coca-Cola Bottling Company of Los Angeles Political Action Committee (CCLA PAC). My understanding of the situation is as follows:

CCLA PAC is registered with the Federal Election Commission (FEC) and files monthly reports with the FEC pursuant to the Federal Election Campaign Act. At the time CCLA PAC was established in 1978, it was the intention of the Board of Directors to support only candidates for federal office. However, employees are encouraged to designate specific candidates for their contributions. Occasionally employees will designate California state or local candidates. Your understanding had been that if the CCLA PAC made $500 or more in contributions to California candidates, it would be required to register and report as a recipient committee under the Political Reform Act.

You asked whether the advice contained in my recent letter to Jonathan W. Redding regarding the filing obligations of a federal candidate's authorized committee would also apply to the CCLA PAC. In that letter, I advised that a California federal candidate's authorized committee would not be required to register and file under the Political Reform Act because it had made contributions of over $500 to local Berkeley candidates. This conclusion was based on the fact that requiring filings from the Dellums Committee would not significantly increase the disclosure that was already available.

Mr. Gordon F. Newton
May 26, 1982
Page Two


However, the filing obligations of federal PACs under
the Political Reform Act were not altered by this advice.
Any PAC which contributes $500 or more in any state or
local election becomes a recipient committee and must
register and report pursuant to the Political Reform Act.
This is consistent with the position of the Federal
Election Commission. See FEC Advisory Opinion 1981-18.

We draw a distinction between federal candidates'
committees and federal PACs on two grounds. First, the
Political Reform Act directs the Secretary of State to send
copies of statements of congressional candidates to the
clerk of the county which contains the largest percentage
of the registered voters in the candidate's election
district and to the clerk of the county within which the
candidate resides or in which his or her committee is
domiciled. Government Code Section 84217. Accordingly,
statements of California federal candidates are available
at a state and local level. No similar provision exists
for federal PACs.

Second, in the case of federal candidates, their
primary purpose is to promote their election to federal
office. Although they may occasionally participate in a
state or local election, the Commission has not faced a
situation where imposing the burden of state filings seemed
justified by significantly increased or different
disclosure. Federal PACs, on the other hand, may devote a
substantial portion of their activities to state and local
campaigns. Thus, state disclosure by federal PACs is
necessary to ensure that complete and meaningful disclosure
is made for state and local elections. As I noted in my
letter to Jonathan Redding, these conclusions are subject
to change if either the FEC or the Commission issues a
ruling on the matter.

If I can be of further assistance, please feel free to
contact me.

Very truly yours,

Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:km

CCOLA PAC

Coca-Cola Bottling Company of Los Angeles
1334 South Central Avenue
Los Angeles, California 90021

FPPC

APR 14 9 01 AM '82

April 12, 1982


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95807

Gentlemen:

I am writing on behalf of Coca-Cola Bottling Co. of Los
Angeles Political Action Committee, a separate segregated fund
(PAC) of Coca-Cola Bottling Co. of Los Angeles, which is duly
registered with the Federal Election Commission ("FEC") and which
files monthly reports under the Federal Election Campaign Act of
1971, as amended, with the FEC and the California Secretary of
state.

Our PAC was established on March 25, 1978.  At that
time, it was the intention of the PAC Board of Directors to
support only candidates for federal office.  However, in
solicitations to our employees, we have encouraged participating
employees to designate their contributions for specific
candidates.  Occasionally, employees request that contributions
be made to California state and local candidates.  It had been
our understanding, based upon past informal advice of the
Commission staff, that if a federally registered committee made
$500 or more in contributions to California state and/or local
candidates, it was required to register as a recipient
committee.  It has come to our attention that on September 1,
1981, Diane Maura Fishburn, legal counsel for the Commission,
issued a letter to Jonathan W. Redding in the Berkeley City
Clerk's office which concluded that contributions by a federal
candidate's authorized campaign committee to local Berkeley city
council candidates would not trigger any state reporting
requirements for the federal candidate's campaign committee, even
though the contributions were well in excess of $500.

In light of that advice, I would be interested to learn
if the filing requirements are different for a corporate federal
PAC.  Specifically, does our federal PAC need to be registered as
a state committee and be responsible for state filings on state

Fair Political Practices Commission
April 12, 1982
Page 2




deadlines?  Also, does the fact that contributions to California
candidates have been earmarked by specific contributors, and that
the PAC is acting as a conduit, affect the PAC's potential filing
responsibilities?

     Thank you for your attention to these matters and I
look forward to your prompt response.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Gordon F. Newton
                                        Chairman
